content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
May 28,
1996
Steven F.
Nord
Merced City Attorney
678 West 18th Street
Merced,
CA
95340
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-96-131
Dear Mr.
Nord:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Merced City Councilmember Dorothea Moore regarding her
responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the 1tActt).l
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ocilesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
May Councilmember Moore participate in decisions affecting
clients of her business for which she is arranging private
financing?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-96-13l
Page
2
CONCLUSION
The councilmember may not participate in any decision if she
has a “nexus” in the decision,
or the decision will materially
affect her business or any client of her business who is a source
of income or promised income.
FACTS
Councilinember Moore was appointed by the city council to
serve as director on a private nonprofit corporation that is under
contract with the city to provide economic development services.
You stated that the corporation was not formed by the city or on
behalf of the city,
the city’s only contact with the private
corporation was being the city’s appointment of a councilmember to
act as a voting director of the corporation and the contract.
As a director of the corporation and as a councilmember,
Councilmember Moore receives information about prospective
industrial and commercial businesses that are deciding whether to
locate within the city.
Many of these businesses will seek
financial assistance from the city or from the city redevelopment
agency.
In her private capacity,
Councilmember Moore is in the
business of finding private funding for businesses.
The
businesses may not need city assistance if they are able to get
private funding through Councilmember Moore.
Generally, the
councilmember receives a finder’s fee for linking up a business
with a financing institution.
She only receives the fee if the
private financing is consummated.
Once the private financing is
obtained,
the business would be able to use the financing either
for a project in Merced or for any other location.
In some cases, private financing may be pending when the
business appears before the council.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California
by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial
manner,
free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)


Our File No.
A-96--l31
Page
3
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
2
A “public official” is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of a state or local
government agency.
This definition would include members of the
Merced City Council.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
A public official has a financial interest in
a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official,
a member of his or her immediate family,
or on any of the following:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
•
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promisedt6 the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
2
Section 87100 would not restrict the councilmember’s private
endeavors so long as they do not constitute making, participating
in making,
or otherwise using her official position to influence a
governmental decision.


Our File No.
A—96-131
Page
4
Business Interests:
The councilmember has an economic
interest in her business pursuant to Section 87103(a)
and
(d)..
Consequently, the councilmember may not make, participate in
making,
or influence any decision which will have a material
financial effect on her business.
Sources of Income:
Section 87103(c) provides that any person
or business that has been a source of income to the councilmeiuber
of $250 or more within the past 12 months, is considered a
potentially disqualifying economic interest.
You stated that the councilmember is in the business of
finding private funding for businesses and that she receives a fee
if the private financing is consummated.
In many cases,
the
private financing is pending when the client appears before the
city council requesting public financing.
The payment is in the
form of a finder’s fee paid by the institution.
Regulation 18704.3 provides special rules for determining who
are the sources of commission income earned in a given
transaction.
Regulation 18704.3 provides:
(a)
This section contains the disclosure and
disqualification requirements for any public
official who receives commission income for
services rendered as an insurance broker or agent,
a real estate broker or agent,
a travel agent or
salesperson,
a stockbroker or a retail or wholesale
salesperson.
(b)
“Commission income” means gross payments
received as a result of services rendered as a
broker,
agent,
or other salesperson for a specific
sale or similar transaction.
Commission income is
received when it is paid or credited.
The finder’s fee that thecouncilmember receives for bringing
the client and the bank together for the private financing is in
essence a commission payment received to act as a broker in the


Our File No.
A—96-131
Page
5
transaction.
3
Regulation 18704.3(d) provides
For purposes of determining whether
disqualification is required under the provisions
of Sections 87100 and 87103(c), the full gross
value of any commission income for a specific sale
or similar transaction shall be attributed to each
source of income in that sale or transaction.
The councilinember’s occupation is not expressly set forth in
the regulation.
However,
by analogy, where the councilmember
receives a fee from a bank for arranging the private financing for
her client through that bank,
both the bank and the client will be
considered the source of that income and the full gross value is
attributed to each.
Finally, please note that sources of promised income are also
economic interests.
For example, generally commission income is
deemed “promised” income when the sale is pending (i.e., the sale
is in escrow),
even prior to the commission actually being paid..
(Felts Advice Letter,
No.
A-85-130; Robbins Advice Letter,
No.
A-
87—074.)
Foreseeability and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Please note that where a decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on jjy economic interest,
a conflict
of interest may exist.
In determining whether a financial effect
on an economic interest,
such as the councilmember’s business,
will be foreseeable, effectsneed only be substantial likely.
Whether income has been actually received or promised is not
determinative of foreseeability.
Webster’s
New World Dictionary: Third College Edition
(1988)
defines broker as “a person who acts as an agent or intermediary
in negotiating contracts,
buying and selling, etc.”
A “finder” is
defined as
“a person,
who for a fee (finder’s fee), initiates a
business deal between others.”


Our File No.
A-96-l31
Page
6
The standard to determine materiality differs depending on
whether a source of income is directly or indirectly involved in a
decision.
Generally, if the official’s source of income is
directly involved
4 in a decision, the effect is deemed to be
material.
Thus,
for example, if a client who is a source of
income or promised income appears before the city council on any
matter that will have a financial effect on the client, the
couñcilmember may not participate in the decision.
Moreover,
the councilmember’s interests are considered
directly affected by a decision where there exists a nexus between
the purpose for which the councilmember receives income and the
governmental decision.
A nexus exists if the councilmember
receives income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated,
aided,
or hindered by the governmental
decision.
5
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1).)
In all other cases, the councilmember is still required to
determine if the indirect effect on her economic interests will be
material.
For example,
the councilmember’s business will nt be
directly involved in the proceeding as set forth in Regulation
18702.1.
However, it may be indirectly affected by the decision.
Regulation 18702.2 provides that for a relatively small business
entity,
the indirect effect of a decision is material where:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more;
or
A client is directly before the city council when the client
initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal,
or similar request,
or is a named party in,
or the subject of, the
proceeding.
A client is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of
any license, permit,
or other entitlement to,
or contract with,
the client.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
For example,
in the Scheidig Advice Letter,
No. A-82-2l2,
the
mayor of Concord received income from the Bay Area Council
(“BAC”)
for work with BAC’s Housing Advocacy Program.
We advised that he
was disqualified from housing decisions coming before the Concord
City Council on which
BAC had taken a particular position,
had a
specific policy or position,
or had a general policy that clearly
implied a specific result on a decision.


Our File No.
A-96-131
Page
7
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Consequently, if subdivision
(g)
is the applicable standard,
the councilmember could not participate in any decision which
would affect the gross revenues of her business by $10,000 or more
for a fiscal year,
or the value of assets or liabilities by
$10,000 or more.
Moreover, if the decision will result in the
business incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$2,500 or more, the councilmember may not participate.
Finally, the Act also provides a “public generally” exception
to its conflict-of-interest requirements.
Your facts do not
indicate that a large segment of the public will be affected in
the same manner as the councilmember’s interests.
We have
enclosed a copy of the regulation for your review.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5660.6
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Geieral Counsel
(
By:
John W.
Wallace
-
counsel,
Legal Division
ENCLOSURE
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

affect her business or any client of her business who is a source
of income or promised income.
